title: Example from Readme fails with TypeError
body:
For me running the example from the Readme file results in:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
[...]
    first_name = container['Names'][0]
TypeError: 'Container' object is not subscriptable
```

It seems that `Container` does not implement `__getitem__(self, key)`. However, I think probably `container.name` was meant to be used.

I think it would also be good to clarify why

```
container = client.containers.get(first_name)
```

is used in the example, as it seems to return the same container as already retrieved from the list. Perhaps, the line could be omitted or a clarifying comment or an `assert` showing that both objects are equal could be added.

Edit: for the problem to show a container must be running, since otherwise the loop will not be entered.

Python: 3.10.12
podman==4.9.0 (podman-py)
OS: Ubuntu 22.04

comments.body:
I misunderstood the equality of the container objects. The following works for me.

```
    for container in client.containers.list():
        container_fetched = client.containers.get(container.name)
        assert container = container_fetched
```

However, then e.g. only `container_fetched.status` works while `container.status` fails with 

```
     ---
     status = get_status(container.status)
  File "[...]/python3.10/site-packages/podman/domain/containers.py", line 54, in status
    return self.attrs["State"]["Status"]
TypeError: string indices must be integers
```

Therefore, I would now rather expect the Readme file to mention that and why a container has to be retrieved again and maybe explicitly highlight that both object are not the same.
